A foundational OpenGL rendering exercise loading OBJ models with Phong lighting and a simple three-point light setup.

This is one of the more foundational pieces in the portfolio: load a model, light it clearly, and understand the rendering path well enough to keep iterating.

Even if it stays archived, it still tells a useful story about progression and the base layer that later graphics projects grew from.

An OpenGL rendering study centered on texture pipelines, lighting passes, and assembling a more complex final frame.

This project was a useful excuse to go deeper on how multiple texture and lighting inputs combine into a final rendered image.

It is the kind of work that gets stronger when the write-up includes frame breakdowns, G-buffer views, and notes about what went wrong before the pipeline became stable.

An ongoing experiment in learning machine learning through geometry processing, retopology, and artist-friendly Python tooling.

This project is an attempt to learn machine learning by building around a problem space I care about: clean geometry, artist workflows, and rig-ready meshes.

The current pass focuses on understanding the pipeline end to end. That means dataset prep, simple experiments, and learning how libraries such as PyTorch, Trimesh, and graph-based tooling fit together.

## Why this matters

I like projects that force multiple disciplines to overlap. This one sits in the middle of graphics engineering, technical art, and ML experimentation, which makes it a good long-term anchor for the portfolio.

## What this page should communicate

As you migrate more of the Wix content over, this page can grow into a full case study with process screenshots, milestones, model results, and reflections on what changed between iterations.

A basic 2D procedural level generator in Unity focused on repeatable rules, layout variation, and practical iteration.

This was a compact procedural generation prototype built to test whether simple rules could quickly produce interesting 2D layouts.

Projects like this are strongest when they show the generation rules, sample outputs, and where human tuning still beats automation. The new site is structured to make those comparisons easy to publish.

A lightweight particle system in a custom 2D engine exploring compute-driven updates, simple vector motion, and orbit behaviors.

This project focused on the basics: vector motion, orbit behaviors, and a compact particle feature set inside a custom engine context.

The portfolio version of the page should eventually show how the data is updated, why compute was useful here, and what constraints shaped the implementation.

A systems-oriented prototype showing how the command pattern can drive animations, sequences, and reusable gameplay-style behaviors.

This project explores how a command-driven architecture can make animation logic easier to sequence, interrupt, and reuse.

Instead of hardwiring one-off flows, the goal is to compose small actions into larger moments. That makes the system more inspectable and easier to extend as the project grows.

## Good future additions

This page would benefit from diagrams, timelines, or before-and-after implementation notes. The new site structure leaves plenty of room for those without changing templates.

A short material study focused on building convincing bark detail and surface breakup in Substance Designer.

This material study was a focused exercise in making bark feel layered, directional, and useful in a real game environment.

The final result matters, but the more useful part of the project is the breakdown: height shaping, secondary breakup, roughness decisions, and how the graph stays readable while the material becomes more detailed.

## Migration note

This page is ready for higher-resolution screenshots or texture callouts in `public/images/projects/` once you move the original media out of Wix.

First Notes on Owning the Site Again
Why moving off a page builder matters, and what a file-driven portfolio/blog setup makes possible.

The portfolio pages and the blog serve different jobs, so separating them inside the same static site feels like the right balance.

Projects need structure and consistency. The blog can be looser. It can hold process thoughts, short notes, and things that are useful to remember later even when they are not polished enough to become a full case study.

That separation is the real upgrade here, not just the tech stack.

Keeping a Lab Notebook for Graphics Work
A separate blog is a good place to keep rendering notes, failed experiments, and small learnings that would clutter project pages.

One reason personal blogs are still useful is that they give experiments a home before they become portfolio pieces.

For graphics and technical art work, that matters a lot. Half the interesting learning happens in small dead ends, partial wins, and test scenes that never deserve a polished launch page on their own.

A dedicated writing section lets those notes stay visible without diluting the portfolio.
